access.accessMethod: Read the checked-out CME submodule path
access.entityLabel: General Corpus source XML for Juliana

entities.description: The checked-in XML source used by this repository, containing editorial front matter, two serialized prose witness streams, a five-line later Bodley addition, and a separate Ashmole verse life.
entities.label: General Corpus source XML for Juliana

rights.basis: The prose work was composed around the end of the twelfth century and the verse work is attested in a manuscript of 1300–1325, centuries before modern copyright terms.
rights.component: The two underlying anonymous medieval works and their medieval witness readings, excluding modern editing, encoding, translation, and photography
rights.jurisdiction: United States
notes: This does not authorize reuse of a modern edition, XML encoding, catalog prose, manuscript photograph, or provider interface.

rights.basis: No repository-level license was found that independently resolves the conflict between U-M's current no-copyright statement and the restrictive availability paragraph embedded in the XML.
rights.component: The checked-in XML artifact, including inherited U-M markup and repository-specific history
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse

rights.component: The U-M Juliana digital item as currently presented
rights.jurisdiction: United States
notes: This current provider statement conflicts in practical scope with the more restrictive availability paragraph embedded in the 1999 XML; both dated layers are retained.

entities.description: U-M Humanities Text Initiative electronic text created in 1999 by keyboarding the 1957 EETS reprint, then restructuring its parallel prose texts as successive divisions.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME item Juliana
notes: The header credits keyboarding to ‘Chrisian’ and claims an error rate no greater than one error per 20,000 characters; no fuller identity is supplied.
The header's sole Latin language declaration is incomplete: the principal medieval texts are Middle English.

rights.basis: The current item page says the materials are in the public domain and supplies the DPLA statement No Copyright – United States.
rights.component: The U-M Juliana digital item as currently presented
rights.jurisdiction: United States
notes: This current provider statement conflicts in practical scope with the more restrictive availability paragraph embedded in the 1999 XML; both dated layers are retained.

rights.basis: The embedded statement permits individual copying for personal use, research, and teaching with the notice, and permits links including from for-profit editions, but expressly forbids redistribution by commercial or nonprofit content providers.
rights.component: The 1999 machine-readable text under the availability statement embedded in its header
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse
notes: Because the current U-M page now states public domain/No Copyright – United States, ask U-M which statement governs redistribution of the XML encoding before relying on either alone.

access.accessMethod: Use the exact CiNii Books record to identify the 1957 OUP reprint and participating library holdings
access.entityLabel: Cockayne and Brock, Þe Liflade of St. Juliana, 1957 OUP reprint
notes: No lawful public full scan of the exact 1957 carrier was verified in this review; the U-M text is not a page-image facsimile.

bibliographic.citation: Þe Liflade of St. Juliana: From Two Old English Manuscripts of 1230 A.D., with Renderings into Modern English, by O. Cockayne and Edmund Brock, edited by Oswald Cockayne (London: Oxford University Press for the Early English Text Society, 1957), EETS Original Series 51; reprint of the 1872 edition.
bibliographic.publisher: Oxford University Press for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 51
bibliographic.title: Þe Liflade of St. Juliana: From Two Old English Manuscripts of 1230 A.D.

entities.description: The immediate printed source identified by U-M. Its encoded title leaf names Oxford University Press in London, New York, and Toronto, and its verso carries Amen House and global-office matter absent from the cached 1872 Trübner states while retaining the inherited 1872 date.
entities.label: Cockayne and Brock, Þe Liflade of St. Juliana, 1957 OUP reprint
notes: The evidence establishes a distinct 1957 reprint carrier but does not establish whether it was produced photographically, from standing type, from plates, or by another process.

rights.basis: The inherited 1872 content is public domain in the United States, but the extent and status of any new 1957 matter have not been established from a complete inspected copy.
rights.component: Any original 1957 publisher matter or alterations distinct from the inherited 1872 edition
rights.jurisdiction: United States and United Kingdom

access.entityLabel: Cockayne and Brock, Þe Liflade of St. Juliana, 1872
notes: The two complete Internet Archive scan states, not this duplicate viewer, were retained in the local cache.

bibliographic.citation: Þe Liflade of St. Juliana: From Two Old English Manuscripts of 1230 A.D., with Renderings into Modern English, by the Rev. O. Cockayne and Edmund Brock, edited by the Rev. Oswald Cockayne (London: N. Trübner for the Early English Text Society, 1872), EETS Original Series 51.
bibliographic.publisher: N. Trübner for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 51
bibliographic.title: Þe Liflade of St. Juliana: From Two Old English Manuscripts of 1230 A.D.

entities.description: The original printed edition: Royal and Bodley prose recensions on facing pages with modern-English renderings beneath, followed by a five-line Bodley addition, a 228-line verse life from Ashmole 43, glossary, and corrections.
entities.label: Cockayne and Brock, Þe Liflade of St. Juliana, 1872
notes: Cockayne's preface says the left-hand text was edited by Brock. The physical arrangement and headings identify this as the Royal text on even pages; Cockayne edited the Bodley stream and the collection generally.

The printed responsibility statement and contents assign the modern-English renderings to Cockayne and Brock: Brock edited and rendered the Royal stream, while Cockayne edited and rendered the Bodley stream. Those translations belong to this source edition but were expressly omitted from the U-M/General Corpus item.
The 1872 title's ‘Old English’ is historical terminology; current catalogs classify the witness texts as Middle English.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1872 and is far outside the maximum U.S. term for works first published before 1978.
rights.component: The 1872 edition's original editorial text, translations, arrangement, glossary, notes, and typography
rights.jurisdiction: United States
notes: This status concerns the historical edition content, not necessarily modern scan files or provider terms.

dateStatements.value: First half of the thirteenth century

entities.description: Composite parchment codex whose early-thirteenth-century part contains Katherine Group works; the prose Life of Saint Juliana occupies ff. 56r–70r.

holding.notes: Life of Saint Juliana at ff. 56r–70r; consult in the British Library's London reading rooms.

entities.label: British Library Royal MS 17 A XXVII

physicalDescription.extent: ff. i + 97, plus unfoliated paper flyleaves; relevant early part ff. 1–70; Juliana ff. 56r–70r
notes: The relevant part is in Middle English associated with the West Midlands / AB-language manuscript group.

access.accessMethod: Complete the mediated-copying order form for reference-quality PDF scans; identify MS. Bodl. 34 and fols. 36v–52r, and ask first if complete-manuscript scanning or foliation is uncertain
access.format: Reference-quality PDF scans with basic shelfmark/folio metadata
notes: The current page states £1 per scan for non-Oxford users and an average turnaround of 10 working days, subject to legal, conservation, and other exceptions.
Images are supplied under Imaging Services terms; request written terms before repository publication or commercial print-on-demand reuse.

rights.basis: The mediated-copying page supplies scans under Imaging Services Terms of Use and does not grant item-specific repository-publication or commercial-reuse permission.
rights.component: Any scans supplied by Bodleian mediated copying or Imaging Services
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and intended place of reuse
notes: Ask for written permission before committing supplied scans or using them in a print-on-demand publication.

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.value: First half of the thirteenth century, perhaps c. 1240

entities.description: Small parchment Katherine Group codex copied by one main scribe; the prose Life of Saint Juliana occupies ff. 36v–52r and loses text with a missing leaf between fols. 40 and 41.

holding.notes: Seinte Iuliene at ff. 36v–52r; five-line late-sixteenth-century verse paraphrase at fol. 52.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library MS Bodley 34

physicalDescription.extent: i + 81 + i folios, foliated i and 1–80; fol. 20b is an inserted slip; Juliana ff. 36v–52r
physicalDescription.format: One column, 25 long lines; one main early-textualis scribe
notes: The catalog identifies Middle English in the south-west Midlands AB language and suggests a possible Hereford origin with low certainty.

rights.basis: The mediated-copying page supplies scans under Imaging Services Terms of Use and does not grant item-specific repository-publication or commercial-reuse permission.

localCopies.label: Complete Bodleian MS Ashmole 43 IIIF facsimile bundle at 1600-pixel width
notes: ZIP contains the provider manifest, an exact-source inventory, and all 580 JPEG service derivatives requested at width 1600 pixels; these are not preservation masters.
Reused by hardlink from a previously verified byte-identical General Corpus Ashmole 43 bundle; Juliana worktree and main-cache inode/size/SHA-256 were audited as identical.

workPortion.label: South English Legendary Life of Saint Juliana
notes: Direct image inspection confirms that fol. 25r begins the Juliana life after the preceding legend and fol. 28r contains its conclusion before the next saint's life.
The complete bundle also preserves all other manuscript leaves, covers, endleaves, and adjacent works.

rights.basis: The item-level IIIF attribution states Photo © Bodleian Libraries, University of Oxford and applies CC BY-NC 4.0; the Digital Bodleian terms limit use to noncommercial purposes except specified academic uses or separately licensed commercial use.

Commercial print-on-demand or a profit-oriented repository use requires separate permission unless it fits a specifically permitted academic-publication category.

next_steps: Locate and inspect a complete 1957 copy from a holding identified by CiNii or request U-M production documentation.
Compare typographic accidents, title/verso matter, pagination, advertisements, and correction state against both cached 1872 copies.
Do not describe the 1957 reprint as a photographic or plate facsimile without direct evidence.
openQuestions.question: What complete physical or digital copy was U-M's exemplar of the 1957 OUP reprint, and how exactly was that reprint produced from the 1872 edition?

next_steps: Ask British Library Reference Services whether the legacy full surrogate remains internally available and whether a new viewer/IIIF object is planned.
If restored, download and audit the complete codex rather than only ff. 56r–70r, then map the Juliana portion within it.
Until then, use the physical-request or paid digitisation-order routes.
openQuestions.question: When will the historically reported full digital coverage of Royal MS 17 A XXVII be restored, and what is its exact image inventory and reuse statement?

next_steps: Ask Bodleian Special Collections whether an unindexed or staff-only whole-codex digital surrogate exists.
Request reference scans of ff. 36v–52r or, if feasible, the complete codex, together with written reuse terms.
Use the 1960 printed facsimile onsite or through lawful controlled lending for layout comparison.
openQuestions.question: Has Bodley 34 been fully digitized in a current public image service apart from the 1960 printed facsimile and controlled loan copies?

next_steps: Report the discrepancy with the exact item locus ff. 25r–28r and direct IIIF boundary evidence.
Continue using the facsimile, U-M text, 1872 edition, and ARLIMA incipit/explicit for identification until corrected.
openQuestions.question: Will the Bodleian correct the Ashmole 43 Juliana item's incipit and explicit, which currently duplicate the neighboring Life of Julian the Hospitaller?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections for a written interpretation specific to downloading, modifying, and redistributing the XML.
Keep the public-domain underlying texts and 1872 edition separate from possible terms on U-M's encoding.
Do not infer that permission to link from for-profit works is permission to republish the XML.
openQuestions.question: Which U-M statement governs redistribution of the Juliana XML: the current public-domain/No Copyright – United States item statement or the 1999 embedded ban on redistribution by commercial and nonprofit content providers?

next_steps: Inspect a high-resolution image of fol. 52 and compare Cockayne's uncertain ‘A Maidwot (?)’ with the current catalog's ‘Quoth Maidwell.’
Do not assign a named author until the hand and wording are resolved.
openQuestions.question: What is the correct reading of the signature following the late-sixteenth-century five-line addition at Bodley 34 fol. 52?

relations.subjectLabel: Huber and Robertson, The Katherine Group (MS Bodley 34), 2016

reviewNotes: Model two intellectual works, three encoded textual streams, and three physical manuscript witnesses. The Royal and Bodley prose streams are distinct recensions of one prose work; the Ashmole poem is a separate verse work.
The 1957 OUP reprint is the immediate U-M source. The cached Internet Archive PDFs are two original 1872 Trübner states and are supporting prior-edition facsimiles, not scans of the 1957 carrier.
Manuscript content, scholarly editing, digital encoding, modern photography, catalog metadata, and provider contract terms are separate rights layers.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy/version of University of Michigan CME item Juliana. U-M created that electronic text in 1999 from the 1957 Oxford University Press reprint of EETS Original Series 51, not from either cached 1872 scan. The 1957 carrier reproduces Cockayne and Brock's 1872 Trübner edition, which prints one prose Life of Saint Juliana in parallel from British Library Royal MS 17 A XXVII, ff. 56r–70r, and Bodleian MS Bodley 34, ff. 36v–52r, then a separate 228-line South English Legendary verse life from Bodleian MS Ashmole 43, ff. 25r–28r. U-M serializes the two prose streams and omits their modern-English renderings and the glossary. Complete public 1872 edition scans, the complete 580-canvas Ashmole 43 IIIF manuscript, a complete 2016 comparison edition, official Bodleian catalog TEI files, and the controlling U.S. copyright circular are cached locally; no current public complete image source was found for Bodley 34, and the British Library's historically reported full Royal surrogate is currently unavailable.

catalogSummary.title: Þe Liflade of St. Juliana: From Two Old English Manuscripts of 1230 A.D.

catalogingSubject.label: Þe Liflade of St. Juliana: From Two Old English Manuscripts of 1230 A.D.
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloging subject is the exact historical scholarly-edition collection encoded by U-M and copied into General Corpus, not an abstract single medieval work. It contains two anonymous Middle English works about Saint Juliana of Nicomedia: one prose life represented by distinct Royal MS 17 A XXVII and Bodley 34 witness streams, and a separate 228-line verse life from Ashmole 43. The two prose streams are not two authored works, and the Ashmole poem is not a third witness of the same prose work. Modern English editorial matter and brief embedded Latin therefore remain in the collection-level discovery facets; the modern-English translations present in the printed source edition are outside this exact encoded collection because U-M expressly omitted them.

scope.description: First publication of Cockayne and Brock's EETS OS 51 collection

extent.description: Collection metrics: one prose work and one 228-line verse work, plus a five-line later Bodley witness addition preserved inside the prose transmission. The XML has four DIV1 elements plus one nested DIV2, 92 P elements, and 235 L elements: 228 belong to the verse work, five to the later Bodley addition, and two are quotations in the prose preface and are excluded from the verse-line total. The two prose streams are representations of one work and are not double-counted.

formStatements.description: The collection contains one prose work and one verse work. Editorial prose and a five-line later addition are separate layers.
Rhythmic, alliterative, poetic prose. Although early editors compared its rhythm to verse, modern editors classify and lay it out as prose.
A 228-line rhymed South English Legendary life.

scope.description: The 1872/1957 editorial collection and its U-M representation

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML header declares only Latin, but direct inspection and all three manuscript catalogs identify the principal language as Middle English.

scope.description: Both medieval works and all three encoded manuscript streams
Title matter, preface, headings, and editorial notes; the source edition's two modern-English renderings are omitted from U-M
Brief quotations, source references, and formulae in editorial and medieval contexts

notes: The record intentionally counts two intellectual works while preserving three encoded manuscript streams and three physical witnesses.
The Royal and Bodley prose streams are parallel textual witnesses of one work; their differences are not flattened into one reconstructed text.
The five-line Bodley addition is witness history rather than a third medieval work, and the two preface verse lines are editorial quotation.
The U-M language declaration of Latin is incomplete and is not used as the work-level language classification.

subjects.label: Virgin martyrdom, chastity, persecution, and conversion

summaries.value: An editorial collection of two anonymous Middle English hagiographic works about Saint Juliana. The earlier, rhythmic prose Life and Passion survives here in two distinct Katherine Group witness streams from Royal MS 17 A XXVII and Bodley 34. A separate rhymed South English Legendary life is represented from Ashmole 43. Both narratives recount Juliana's refusal of a pagan marriage, torture, confrontation with a devil, martyrdom, and posthumous fate.

The encoding contains Cockayne's preface; the Royal prose stream; the Bodley prose stream followed by a five-line late-sixteenth-century addition; and the 228-line Ashmole verse life. U-M serializes the prose witnesses that were printed on facing pages and omits the two modern-English renderings and glossary.

Do not merge this item with Cynewulf's Old English verse Juliana. The 1872 title's ‘Old English’ is historical terminology for the early Middle English prose witnesses, while the encoded Ashmole poem is a different Middle English work.

notes: This is the historical edition title and therefore names only the two prose manuscripts even though the collection also contains a separate Ashmole verse life.
The title's phrase ‘Old English’ reflects 1872 terminology; the encoded medieval texts are now classified as Middle English.

notes: U-M retains this source-edition title even though its encoding expressly omits both modern-English renderings.

work.summary: An editorial collection of two anonymous Middle English hagiographic works about Saint Juliana. The earlier, rhythmic prose Life and Passion survives here in two distinct Katherine Group witness streams from Royal MS 17 A XXVII and Bodley 34. A separate rhymed South English Legendary life is represented from Ashmole 43. Both narratives recount Juliana's refusal of a pagan marriage, torture, confrontation with a devil, martyrdom, and posthumous fate.
